skill.name: agent-browser
skill.description: Browser automation CLI for AI agents. Use for website interaction, form automation, screenshots, scraping, and web app verification. Prefer snapshot refs (@e1, @e2) for deterministic actions.
skill.summary: Deterministic browser automation CLI with snapshot-based element refs for reliable web workflows.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer flagged 80 external_commands and 10 network patterns, but all are FALSE POSITIVES. The patterns appear in documentation files (.md) showing CLI usage examples, not executable code. The skill is a legitimate browser automation tool with built-in security features including domain allowlists, action policies, and environment variable-based credential management. No malicious intent detected.

risk_factor_evidence.reasoning: Markdown documentation showing CLI command examples, not actual shell execution. The backticks are markdown code block syntax for user reference.
Documented URLs are legitimate browser automation targets (example.com placeholders) and project resource links. Browser automation inherently requires network access.

low_findings.title: External Command Documentation Patterns
low_findings.description: Static analyzer detected shell command patterns in markdown files. After review, these are documentation examples for a CLI tool, not executable code. The skill teaches users how to use the agent-browser CLI safely.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged patterns are in markdown code blocks within documentation files, not in executable scripts or code.

content.user_title: Automate Browser Workflows with AI Agents
content.value_statement: Manual web testing and browser automation require complex scripts and fragile selectors. This skill provides deterministic browser control using snapshot-based element references for reliable, repeatable workflows.
actual_capabilities: Navigate to websites and wait for page stabilization
Capture accessibility tree snapshots with element references
Perform click, fill, type, select, and check actions using @eN refs
Take screenshots and PDFs with optional annotation
Run visual regression tests with baseline image comparison
Manage isolated sessions for parallel browser tasks
limitations: Requires agent-browser CLI tool installed on the system
Element refs become stale after navigation or DOM changes - re-snapshot required
Browser automation inherently requires network access to target websites
Not suitable for CAPTCHA-protected or heavily bot-detected sites

use_cases.title: Web Application Testing
use_cases.description: Verify UI changes, test form submissions, and validate user flows with deterministic element references that survive DOM updates.
use_cases.target_user: QA engineers and developers testing web applications

use_cases.title: Automated Data Extraction
use_cases.description: Navigate to websites, extract structured content using scoped snapshots, and capture results as screenshots or structured data.
use_cases.target_user: Researchers and analysts collecting web data

use_cases.title: Visual Regression Monitoring
use_cases.description: Capture baseline screenshots, automate user interactions, and detect visual changes with annotated diff reporting.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Page Navigation
prompt_templates.prompt: Open https://example.com, wait for the page to load, and show me the accessibility snapshot with element references.

prompt_templates.title: Form Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Navigate to the login page, wait for network idle, snapshot the page, fill the email and password fields using refs, click submit, and verify the dashboard loaded.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: Complete a multi-step form workflow

prompt_templates.title: Visual Regression Test
prompt_templates.prompt: Take a baseline screenshot, perform the checkout flow, then compare the final state against the baseline and report any visual differences.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: Automated visual testing with evidence

prompt_templates.title: Parallel Session Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Open two isolated sessions: one for research on example.com and one for shopping on shop.example.com. Run both workflows concurrently and close both sessions when complete.

output: Differences highlighted in annotated output image

best_practices: Always re-snapshot after navigation or DOM changes before using element refs
Use explicit waits (networkidle, selector, ref) instead of fixed sleep delays
Use named sessions for parallel workflows and close them at task completion
anti_patterns: Reusing stale refs after page navigation or significant DOM updates
Embedding credentials directly in scripts instead of using environment variables
Skipping session cleanup which can cause resource leaks and cookie collisions

faq.question: Why do element refs become invalid?
faq.answer: Refs are generated from the accessibility tree snapshot. Any navigation, form submission with rerender, or DOM rebuild invalidates the tree structure, making old refs point to wrong or non-existent elements.

faq.question: How do I handle authentication workflows?
faq.answer: Use environment variables for credentials, perform login once, save session state with 'state save', and reload it for subsequent runs to avoid repeated authentication.

faq.question: Can I run multiple browser sessions simultaneously?
faq.answer: Yes, use the --session flag with unique names for each concurrent task. This isolates cookies, localStorage, and browser context between sessions.

faq.question: What security hardening options are available?
faq.answer: Use AGENT_BROWSER_ALLOWED_DOMAINS for domain allowlisting, AGENT_BROWSER_ACTION_POLICY for action restrictions, and AGENT_BROWSER_CONTENT_BOUNDARIES to reduce prompt injection risk.

faq.question: How do I debug when an interaction fails?
faq.answer: Run snapshot -i again to get fresh refs, verify the element label text matches your target, and use wait commands for dynamic content before interacting.

faq.question: What is the recommended command chaining pattern?
faq.answer: Use && chaining for simple sequences where intermediate output is not needed. Separate commands when you need to parse output or refs before the next action.
